Patch Set 1: Code-Review-1

(1 comment)

I also wonder if cdt_state test/updates must not need to be protected against racy scenarios?

Patch Set 1:

(1 comment)

> I also wonder if cdt_state test/updates must not need to be
 > protected against racy scenarios?

Yes, there is at least one that I saw. When the cancel all command is used, the cdt move to the disabled state and eventually restores it to whatever it was before. But if we stop it in the mean time, the old state is still restored, so strange things might happen. However it's not what I was trying to fix, so I left that on the back burner.

Patch Set 24:

This might have been merged a bit too early, how do we handle this?

I suppose reverting the commit just to reintroduce it under the right LU is a bit too extreme... I can do an almost no-op patch just to reference LU-9063: the change would be to modify the way cdt_state_transition is declared, and its commit message would reference this patch and how it is related to LU-9063. What do you think?

commit.subject: LU-7988 hsm: change coordinator start/stop mechanisms
commit.message:
LU-7988 hsm: change coordinator start/stop mechanisms

Instead of using cdt_state and cdt_thread.t_flags to keep the state of
the coordinator, only use cdt_state. cdt_thread.t_flags is now only
used to signal the coordinator, not to stop it. cdt_state is used to
control the coordinator behaviour.

Split mdt_hsm_cdt_stop() into 2 functions. One to actually signal the
coordinator to stop, and another one to cleanup its ressources. The
coordinator is now responsible to clean its own ressources instead of
having two different paths depending on whether Lustre is shutting
down, or a user is stopping it through /proc.

Protect cdt_state with a spin lock, and add a transition table to
catch invalid transitions.

Removed mdt_opts.mo_coordinator as it is just a subset of cdt_state.

Test-Parameters: trivial testlist=sanity-hsm
